text: In this paper we focus on manifest interparty conflict concerning policy issues and the potential role of coalition agreements in resolving them. Based on information about policy issues in coalition agreements from Germany, Belgium, Italy and the Netherlands, and on data about the extent of intracoalition conflict during the lives of eleven different West European coalition governments, we investigate what characterises policy mandating in such agreements (i.e. what kind of policy issues are included, and what form of agreements concerning them are produced) and how frequently in the course of a term coalition parties must contend with each other on the issues included. Our empirical analyses provide the following information: (a) all four types of Western European democracy have policy mandating in the form of coalition agreements containing considerable detail of policy content, which is, however, specified with exactness in only about one third of the cases included in our analyses; and (b) in each of the four cases, in almost all instances where intracoalition conflicts over policy issues were registered during the lifetime of a particular coalition, the relevant policy provisions from the parties' coalition agreement had to be referred to in the course of settling the conflicts. In short, all four examples of West European democracies show that policy mandating by means of formal coalition agreements may induce parties in a governing coalition to refrain from manifest conflict in the course of a consequence that makes the nature of policy mandating in such agreements anything but that of 'cheap talk'.
headline: Fully Human Written
